protocol.failureScoring: inconclusive is always incorrect for accuracy; inconclusive positives count as false negatives; completion is reported separately
protocol.primaryReporting: per-run metrics plus mean/range and cross-run stability; repetitions are not independent samples
protocol.selection: all cases, all three repetitions, all misses and errors; no best-run selection

changeControl.reportingOnlyFix: Preserve a successful embedding pair's null error instead of serializing it as the string 'missing'.

judgeVerdict.reason: Both statements make opposing universal claims about the same behavior (timeliness of supplier invoice payments) — 'on time' vs. 'chronically late' — and cannot both be true.
Both statements assert opposite states about the same invoice (ZX-41): 'settled in full' vs 'outstanding in full' — mutually exclusive conditions.
Both statements make opposing claims about the same subject (revenue change for the frozen reporting period) — 'increased' vs 'decreased' — which cannot both be true.
Both statements assert opposite outcomes (approved vs. rejected) for the same credit request CR-8; they cannot both be true.
Both statements assert mutually exclusive states (active vs. terminated) about the same customer account C-18 at the same logical time; an account cannot be both active and terminated simultaneously.
Both statements refer to the same signed review and assert opposite financial statuses (solvent vs. insolvent) for the same entity (Oriole Ltd); they cannot both be true.
Both statements make opposing truth claims about the same supplier (S-7) and same control (C4): one asserts compliance, the other asserts breach — mutually exclusive states.
The statements make opposing universal claims about the same behavior (punctuality of invoice payments by Vendor Aster): 'never late' vs 'repeatedly late' — logically incompatible.
Both statements make opposing truth claims about the same invoice's outstanding status at the same logical time; 'not outstanding' and 'still outstanding' are direct logical negations.
Both statements refer to unit volume in the same weekly window but assert opposite directions (rose vs. fell); they cannot both be true simultaneously.
Both statements make opposing claims about the current activity status of the same subscription (S-20): 'remains active' vs 'was cancelled and is no longer active' — mutually exclusive states.
Both statements assert opposite outcomes (accepted vs. denied) for the same application (A-91) by the same authority (the committee); they cannot both be true.
A debt cannot both be paid and defaulted before the same cutoff; these are mutually exclusive states.
Reliable and delinquent are logically opposite assessments of payer behavior in the same audit; cannot both be true simultaneously.
Both statements assert opposite compliance statuses for the same control owner (O-2) at the same point in time (the review date); they cannot both be true.
Both statements assert opposite financial outcomes (surplus vs. deficit) for the same event (Project Atlas closing) and cannot both be true.
Both statements refer to the same subject (June sales in the board pack) and assert opposite trends (growth vs. decline), which cannot both be true.
Both statements assert opposite arrival timing relative to the same promised date for SH-4: 'two days before' vs 'three days after' — mutually exclusive.
Both statements make opposing claims about the same contract (K-2) and same subject (subcontractors for this work package): one asserts permission, the other prohibition — logically incompatible.
Both statements refer to the same policy (P-6) and the same requirement (record retention duration), but assert incompatible durations: 30 days vs. 90 days.
Both statements refer to the same subject (Berlin office weekday customer access) and assert incompatible opening times: 08:00 vs. not before 10:00.
Both statements make opposing claims about the same warranty (W-3) and the same item (replacement batteries): one asserts inclusion, the other explicit exclusion — logically incompatible.
Both statements make opposing claims about whether VAT is included in the displayed total of the same quote (Q-55); they cannot both be true.
Both statements affirm timely payment behavior; 'pays supplier invoices on time' and 'reliable and punctual payer' are consistent and mutually supportive.
A paid invoice is by definition not outstanding; the statements are logically consistent and mutually supportive.
Profit and surplus are synonymous in many business contexts (especially accounting/finance), both indicating positive net financial outcome; no direct opposition in meaning.
Both statements assert a positive outcome for application C-5; 'approved' and 'accepted' are synonymous in this context and do not oppose each other.
Both statements assert that Account T-3 is in an active state; 'no longer inactive' logically implies 'active' in a binary active/inactive schema.
Outstanding on Monday morning and settled on Friday afternoon are temporally compatible; an invoice can be outstanding early in the week and settled later.
Statements refer to different time periods (before vs. after merger); an account can be active before and terminated after — no logical inconsistency.
Statements refer to different time periods (before vs. after rule change); compliance status can legally and logically change over time.
The statements describe different attributes (arrival day vs. carton count) of the same shipment; no logical or factual opposition exists.
Statements refer to different invoices (I-1 and I-2), so they cannot contradict each other.
Statements refer to different accounts (A vs B) and different statuses (active vs terminated); no shared subject or opposing claim.
Both statements assert that Invoice J-1 is paid/settled; 'paid' and 'settled' are synonymous in this financial context.
Both statements make opposing universal claims about the same behavior (timeliness of supplier invoice payments) — 'on time' vs. 'chronically late' — and cannot both be true.

aggregate.note: Per-run mean/range only; repeated runs are not treated as independent samples.

caveats: Synthetic, hand-authored business-memory pairs are not a production prevalence sample.
Confidence is an ordinal model output, not a calibrated probability.
Offline vectors isolate judge behavior; online vectors exercise text-embedding-v4 subject gating.
Online embeddings are frozen once and reused across repetitions, matching production persisted-vector reuse.
All cases, misses, errors and repetitions are retained; no run is selected or discarded.
Each pair is evaluated in isolation. This measures subject gating plus opposition judgement, not full-corpus O(n²) candidate ranking or maxPairs truncation.
The 48 labels are synthetic and developer-authored before the online run; they are not independent expert annotations.
Detector/judge p50 and p95 exclude the one-time embedding step; embedding latency is reported separately.
Three repetitions measure output stability on one frozen set; they are not 144 independent samples.
Protocol v1.1 changes reporting only: it preserves null for successful embedding pairs. Dataset, labels, model, thresholds, calls, scoring and selection are unchanged from v1.
The contradictory v1 artifact is retained verbatim at bench/results/semantic-heldout-qwen-v1-reporting-bug.json; v1.1 never overwrites it.
